channel.title: Best Mortgages For You – UK Property & Mortgage News
channel.description: Latest UK property and mortgage news from leading industry blogs.

item.title: Need Help Understanding the Renters Rights Act? Here Are Your Training Options
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Understanding the Renters Reform Act: Training Options Available

With the Renters Reform Act now in effect, landlords, letting agents and housing professionals may wish to ensure they're fully informed about the changing legal requirements. Several training options exist to help stakeholders navigate these reforms, ranging from free introductory courses to bespoke in-house sessions.

Free and Paid Learning Resources

For those new to renting regulations or needing a refresher, a free 12-part online course covers essential landlord and tenant law. More comprehensive training comes through a series of 16 monthly webinars addressing multiple aspects of property letting, including:

  • Pre-tenancy preparations
  • Property standards compliance
  • Local authority enforcement procedures
  • Tenancy agreement best practices
  • Possession proceedings guidance

These live sessions allow for participant questions and offer recorded access for twelve months afterwards. While included in certain membership packages, non-members might consider paying £18 per webinar, with continuing professional development certificates available upon request.

Specialist Training Solutions

A three-hour masterclass recording remains accessible through business membership portals, detailing all Renters Reform Act changes. Organisations requiring tailored training might explore customised programmes delivered either by housing law specialists or qualified barristers.

From fundamental courses to specialist sessions, these learning opportunities could help property professionals maintain compliance and confidence during this period of legislative change.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Understanding the Renters Reform Act: Training Options Available

With the Renters Reform Act now in effect, landlords, letting agents and housing professionals may wish to ensure they're fully informed about the changing legal requirements. Several training options exist to help stakeholders navigate these reforms, ranging from free introductory courses to bespoke in-house sessions.

Free and Paid Learning Resources

For those new to renting regulations or needing a refresher, a free 12-part online course covers essential landlord and tenant law. More comprehensive training comes through a series of 16 monthly webinars addressing multiple aspects of property letting, including:

  • Pre-tenancy preparations
  • Property standards compliance
  • Local authority enforcement procedures
  • Tenancy agreement best practices
  • Possession proceedings guidance

These live sessions allow for participant questions and offer recorded access for twelve months afterwards. While included in certain membership packages, non-members might consider paying £18 per webinar, with continuing professional development certificates available upon request.

Specialist Training Solutions

A three-hour masterclass recording remains accessible through business membership portals, detailing all Renters Reform Act changes. Organisations requiring tailored training might explore customised programmes delivered either by housing law specialists or qualified barristers.

From fundamental courses to specialist sessions, these learning opportunities could help property professionals maintain compliance and confidence during this period of legislative change.

item.title: MPs urge government to accelerate and strengthen leasehold reform
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

MPs push for stronger leasehold reforms

Cross-party MPs have urged the government to bolster its proposed leasehold reforms, arguing current plans don’t go far enough to protect homeowners. The Housing, Communities and Local Government Committee’s report welcomes progress in the draft Commonhold and Leasehold Reform Bill but highlights key areas requiring tougher action.

Key recommendations for reform

The committee’s proposals focus on three main areas:

  • Ground rent cap: While supporting the proposed £250 annual cap, MPs recommend accelerating implementation to 2027 rather than the government’s suggested timeline.
  • Faster transition periods: The report questions why ground rents should take 40 years to phase out completely when a 20-year transition might better balance interests between leaseholders and investors.
  • Property management regulation: The committee calls for establishing an independent regulator with powers to sanction poor-performing agents, including revoking operating licences.

Addressing long-standing issues

The report criticises historical failures in addressing unfair leasehold practices, noting many homeowners face excessive service charges and limited control over their properties. Committee chair Florence Eshalomi MP stated the reforms represent progress but emphasised the need for stronger consumer protections against unreasonable fees.

With leasehold reform now moving through Parliament, homeowners may wish to monitor developments as these changes could significantly affect property ownership costs and rights in coming years.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

MPs push for stronger leasehold reforms

Cross-party MPs have urged the government to bolster its proposed leasehold reforms, arguing current plans don’t go far enough to protect homeowners. The Housing, Communities and Local Government Committee’s report welcomes progress in the draft Commonhold and Leasehold Reform Bill but highlights key areas requiring tougher action.

Key recommendations for reform

The committee’s proposals focus on three main areas:

  • Ground rent cap: While supporting the proposed £250 annual cap, MPs recommend accelerating implementation to 2027 rather than the government’s suggested timeline.
  • Faster transition periods: The report questions why ground rents should take 40 years to phase out completely when a 20-year transition might better balance interests between leaseholders and investors.
  • Property management regulation: The committee calls for establishing an independent regulator with powers to sanction poor-performing agents, including revoking operating licences.

Addressing long-standing issues

The report criticises historical failures in addressing unfair leasehold practices, noting many homeowners face excessive service charges and limited control over their properties. Committee chair Florence Eshalomi MP stated the reforms represent progress but emphasised the need for stronger consumer protections against unreasonable fees.

With leasehold reform now moving through Parliament, homeowners may wish to monitor developments as these changes could significantly affect property ownership costs and rights in coming years.

item.title: Rogue agent and landlord ordered to repay more than £90k
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

A landlord and property management firm in east London have been ordered to repay over £90,000 following a council investigation into dangerous living conditions and unlawful activities at a rental home in Wanstead. The substantial repayment was issued under the Proceeds of Crime Act after authorities discovered multiple breaches at a five-bedroom property, including alleged drug cultivation and illegal electricity bypassing.

Council investigation uncovers multiple hazards

Redbridge Council launched an investigation following reports about the property, with Metropolitan Police later confirming criminal activity at the address. Initial inspections revealed the house empty but in poor condition, prompting officials to require remedial works before legal occupation. However, subsequent visits uncovered ten unrelated men living at the property despite its unsafe state.

Housing enforcement officers identified five category 1 hazards – the most serious classification under the Housing Act 2004 – alongside numerous other safety concerns. The council issued a Prohibition Order banning occupancy until repairs were completed, yet the landlord and agent allegedly continued renting the property for over a year in breach of this order.

Zero tolerance approach to rogue operators

The case reached Snaresbrook Crown Court, where property owner Chad Miah and All Season Lettings and Management faced proceedings under the Proceeds of Crime Act. The court ordered them to pay £91,068 in combined fines, costs and confiscation orders.

Councillor Saima Ahmed, Redbridge Council's cabinet member for housing, emphasised their strict stance: "This outcome demonstrates our commitment to protecting residents from exploitative practices. While we support responsible landlords, we'll use the full force of the law against those who endanger tenants for profit."

The council highlights this case as part of wider efforts to improve standards in the private rented sector. Authorities encourage tenants to report poor conditions through official channels, with enforcement teams prioritising investigations into the most serious violations.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

A landlord and property management firm in east London have been ordered to repay over £90,000 following a council investigation into dangerous living conditions and unlawful activities at a rental home in Wanstead. The substantial repayment was issued under the Proceeds of Crime Act after authorities discovered multiple breaches at a five-bedroom property, including alleged drug cultivation and illegal electricity bypassing.

Council investigation uncovers multiple hazards

Redbridge Council launched an investigation following reports about the property, with Metropolitan Police later confirming criminal activity at the address. Initial inspections revealed the house empty but in poor condition, prompting officials to require remedial works before legal occupation. However, subsequent visits uncovered ten unrelated men living at the property despite its unsafe state.

Housing enforcement officers identified five category 1 hazards – the most serious classification under the Housing Act 2004 – alongside numerous other safety concerns. The council issued a Prohibition Order banning occupancy until repairs were completed, yet the landlord and agent allegedly continued renting the property for over a year in breach of this order.

Zero tolerance approach to rogue operators

The case reached Snaresbrook Crown Court, where property owner Chad Miah and All Season Lettings and Management faced proceedings under the Proceeds of Crime Act. The court ordered them to pay £91,068 in combined fines, costs and confiscation orders.

Councillor Saima Ahmed, Redbridge Council's cabinet member for housing, emphasised their strict stance: "This outcome demonstrates our commitment to protecting residents from exploitative practices. While we support responsible landlords, we'll use the full force of the law against those who endanger tenants for profit."

The council highlights this case as part of wider efforts to improve standards in the private rented sector. Authorities encourage tenants to report poor conditions through official channels, with enforcement teams prioritising investigations into the most serious violations.

item.title: Gradual Commonhold reform key to safeguarding housing supply
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Balancing Commonhold Reform With Housing Supply Needs

The government's draft legislation to reform leasehold ownership marks a significant shift in property law, with plans to transition new developments to commonhold arrangements. Whilst this fulfils a manifesto commitment to abolish leasehold, experts suggest the changes must be carefully managed to avoid disrupting already sluggish housing delivery targets.

The ambitious pledge to build 1.5 million homes during this Parliament comes at a time when housebuilding rates hover near historic lows. Achieving this target may require greater urban density developments, where flats often represent the only viable solution in land-constrained areas. The success of such schemes could partially depend on property developers' willingness to embrace commonhold models.

Overcoming the Challenges of Commonhold Adoption

Commonhold remains relatively untested across England and Wales, with fewer than 25 developments currently operating under this system. Industry stakeholders have expressed concerns about potential risks, given the limited track record for lenders, investors and developers. The government acknowledges that existing commonhold legislation, introduced in 2004, requires substantial refinement before widespread adoption becomes practical.

Key technical issues requiring attention include:

  • Effective dispute resolution mechanisms
  • Clear processes for amending constitutional documents
  • Provisions for terminating commonhold arrangements
  • Mortgage lender acceptance criteria

As the property market prepares for these changes, gradual implementation may prove crucial to maintaining housing supply whilst allowing time for the commonhold system to develop necessary safeguards. Homeowners considering new-build properties might wish to monitor how these reforms progress, as they could significantly influence future property ownership structures.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Balancing Commonhold Reform With Housing Supply Needs

The government's draft legislation to reform leasehold ownership marks a significant shift in property law, with plans to transition new developments to commonhold arrangements. Whilst this fulfils a manifesto commitment to abolish leasehold, experts suggest the changes must be carefully managed to avoid disrupting already sluggish housing delivery targets.

The ambitious pledge to build 1.5 million homes during this Parliament comes at a time when housebuilding rates hover near historic lows. Achieving this target may require greater urban density developments, where flats often represent the only viable solution in land-constrained areas. The success of such schemes could partially depend on property developers' willingness to embrace commonhold models.

Overcoming the Challenges of Commonhold Adoption

Commonhold remains relatively untested across England and Wales, with fewer than 25 developments currently operating under this system. Industry stakeholders have expressed concerns about potential risks, given the limited track record for lenders, investors and developers. The government acknowledges that existing commonhold legislation, introduced in 2004, requires substantial refinement before widespread adoption becomes practical.

Key technical issues requiring attention include:

  • Effective dispute resolution mechanisms
  • Clear processes for amending constitutional documents
  • Provisions for terminating commonhold arrangements
  • Mortgage lender acceptance criteria

As the property market prepares for these changes, gradual implementation may prove crucial to maintaining housing supply whilst allowing time for the commonhold system to develop necessary safeguards. Homeowners considering new-build properties might wish to monitor how these reforms progress, as they could significantly influence future property ownership structures.

item.title: BTL landlords see sharp rise in rental income despite widespread arrears
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Landlords See Strong Rental Growth Despite Ongoing Arrears Challenges

New research reveals buy-to-let landlords have enjoyed significant rental income growth over the past year, even as many continue to grapple with tenant arrears. The latest market analysis shows average gross rental income per property surged by 22.9% year-on-year, climbing from £9,860 in early 2025 to £12,117 in Q1 2026.

The data highlights considerable regional variations in portfolio performance. The West Midlands emerged as a standout region, recording the strongest growth in both portfolio size and value. Landlords in this area increased their holdings by an average of 3.7 properties, while portfolio values jumped 29.4% to £2.2 million.

Regional Variations in Portfolio Performance

Whilst some regions flourished, others faced declines. Central London and the North East saw the sharpest reductions in average portfolio sizes, each dropping by 1.9 properties compared to the previous year. Portfolio values also fell noticeably in the North West, Yorkshire and Humber, and the South West.

Rental income growth proved particularly robust in the North West, where average income per property leapt by over 52%. The West Midlands, Wales, East of England and Central London also posted strong gains. Only the North East recorded a decrease in rental income per property.

With approximately 2.8 million landlords operating across the UK, the combined value of landlord portfolios now stands at an estimated £4.8 trillion. The average landlord owns 7.3 properties worth around £1.7 million.

Arrears Remain Persistent Challenge

Behind these positive headline figures lies an ongoing challenge for many landlords. Around 30% – equating to roughly 846,000 property owners – have experienced tenant arrears at some point during the past year.

Industry experts suggest the market presents a complex picture for landlords. Whilst rising rents reflect sustained tenant demand and constrained supply, many property investors simultaneously face higher borrowing costs and arrears pressures.

For those considering buy-to-let investments, these findings highlight the importance of thorough research into local market conditions and careful financial planning to account for potential voids or arrears periods.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Landlords See Strong Rental Growth Despite Ongoing Arrears Challenges

New research reveals buy-to-let landlords have enjoyed significant rental income growth over the past year, even as many continue to grapple with tenant arrears. The latest market analysis shows average gross rental income per property surged by 22.9% year-on-year, climbing from £9,860 in early 2025 to £12,117 in Q1 2026.

The data highlights considerable regional variations in portfolio performance. The West Midlands emerged as a standout region, recording the strongest growth in both portfolio size and value. Landlords in this area increased their holdings by an average of 3.7 properties, while portfolio values jumped 29.4% to £2.2 million.

Regional Variations in Portfolio Performance

Whilst some regions flourished, others faced declines. Central London and the North East saw the sharpest reductions in average portfolio sizes, each dropping by 1.9 properties compared to the previous year. Portfolio values also fell noticeably in the North West, Yorkshire and Humber, and the South West.

Rental income growth proved particularly robust in the North West, where average income per property leapt by over 52%. The West Midlands, Wales, East of England and Central London also posted strong gains. Only the North East recorded a decrease in rental income per property.

With approximately 2.8 million landlords operating across the UK, the combined value of landlord portfolios now stands at an estimated £4.8 trillion. The average landlord owns 7.3 properties worth around £1.7 million.

Arrears Remain Persistent Challenge

Behind these positive headline figures lies an ongoing challenge for many landlords. Around 30% – equating to roughly 846,000 property owners – have experienced tenant arrears at some point during the past year.

Industry experts suggest the market presents a complex picture for landlords. Whilst rising rents reflect sustained tenant demand and constrained supply, many property investors simultaneously face higher borrowing costs and arrears pressures.

For those considering buy-to-let investments, these findings highlight the importance of thorough research into local market conditions and careful financial planning to account for potential voids or arrears periods.

item.title: Government-backed property data trial tests future of homebuying
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

A pioneering government-backed trial has explored how secure property data sharing could transform the homebuying process in England and Wales. The Smart Property Data Trust Framework sandbox, funded by a £742,700 grant from the Regulators’ Pioneer Fund, has tested new approaches to verifying and exchanging housing information between approved parties.

Streamlining property transactions

The 12-month initiative, led by the Council for Licensed Conveyancers in partnership with the Open Property Data Association (OPDA), created a controlled environment for testing shared data standards. Unlike current systems that rely on manual document handling or bespoke integrations between individual firms, the framework allows multiple organisations to connect through a single, reusable system.

PropTech firm Kotini participated as an early tester, accessing HM Land Registry data through the sandbox. Founder Kieran Witt described how the traditional approach created unnecessary barriers: "Each new integration required significant time and resources - what we called an 'innovation tax'. This framework removes those repetitive technical hurdles, letting firms focus on improving services rather than administrative overheads."

Potential industry-wide benefits

The trial demonstrated how verified property data could flow securely between different market participants while maintaining clear records of its origin. Maria Harris, OPDA chair, highlighted the collaborative nature of the project: "The sandbox supports the whole ecosystem working together. As it's not a closed environment, any approved organisation can participate in testing data sharing."

Key advantages identified during the trial include:

  • Reduced duplication when accessing core property information
  • Standardised approaches to data verification
  • Potential cost savings across the transaction chain
  • Improved accessibility for smaller property firms

Whilst the sandbox continues operating until later this year, early results suggest such frameworks could help modernise the property market. Homeowners may find future transactions become more efficient as these data-sharing standards develop, potentially reducing delays and paperwork in the homebuying process.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

A pioneering government-backed trial has explored how secure property data sharing could transform the homebuying process in England and Wales. The Smart Property Data Trust Framework sandbox, funded by a £742,700 grant from the Regulators’ Pioneer Fund, has tested new approaches to verifying and exchanging housing information between approved parties.

Streamlining property transactions

The 12-month initiative, led by the Council for Licensed Conveyancers in partnership with the Open Property Data Association (OPDA), created a controlled environment for testing shared data standards. Unlike current systems that rely on manual document handling or bespoke integrations between individual firms, the framework allows multiple organisations to connect through a single, reusable system.

PropTech firm Kotini participated as an early tester, accessing HM Land Registry data through the sandbox. Founder Kieran Witt described how the traditional approach created unnecessary barriers: "Each new integration required significant time and resources - what we called an 'innovation tax'. This framework removes those repetitive technical hurdles, letting firms focus on improving services rather than administrative overheads."

Potential industry-wide benefits

The trial demonstrated how verified property data could flow securely between different market participants while maintaining clear records of its origin. Maria Harris, OPDA chair, highlighted the collaborative nature of the project: "The sandbox supports the whole ecosystem working together. As it's not a closed environment, any approved organisation can participate in testing data sharing."

Key advantages identified during the trial include:

  • Reduced duplication when accessing core property information
  • Standardised approaches to data verification
  • Potential cost savings across the transaction chain
  • Improved accessibility for smaller property firms

Whilst the sandbox continues operating until later this year, early results suggest such frameworks could help modernise the property market. Homeowners may find future transactions become more efficient as these data-sharing standards develop, potentially reducing delays and paperwork in the homebuying process.

item.title: Estate agent turned politician accuses Andy Burnham of hypocrisy over let flat
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

MPs' Property Investments Under Scrutiny

The relationship between politicians and property ownership has come under fresh scrutiny following revelations about Greater Manchester Mayor Andy Burnham’s London flat. The Labour figure purchased the ex-council property in Kennington during his time as an MP in 2005, with reports suggesting partial funding came through parliamentary expenses. The flat, now valued at approximately £480,000, has been rented out whilst Burnham claimed accommodation expenses for another property.

Housing Market Hypocrisy Claims

Conservative MP and former Hunters estate agency founder Kevin Hollinrake has criticised Burnham’s position, labelling it hypocritical given his public stance on housing affordability. Burnham has frequently spoken about growing wealth disparities between property owners and those struggling to enter the market. Hollinrake remarked: "The self-styled King of the North maintains a profitable London property portfolio whilst criticising the very system that enabled his investment."

This situation echoes the 2012 controversy when Burnham, then shadow health secretary, faced questions about claiming rental expenses whilst letting out his own property. The parliamentary expenses system underwent significant reform after the 2009 scandal, prohibiting MPs from claiming mortgage interest but still allowing London accommodation costs.

Right to Buy Paradox

The debate touches upon wider housing policy contradictions. Burnham has advocated for rent controls and called to suspend Right to Buy – the scheme allowing council tenants to purchase their homes at discounts. Ironically, his Kennington flat was originally acquired through Right to Buy before he purchased it. Housing analysts suggest such cases highlight the complex relationship individuals can have with property markets, where personal investments may not always align with public policy positions.

As housing remains a contentious political issue, the financial decisions of policymakers continue to attract public interest and scrutiny. Homeowners considering buy-to-let investments might reflect on how market participation intersects with broader housing affordability debates.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

MPs' Property Investments Under Scrutiny

The relationship between politicians and property ownership has come under fresh scrutiny following revelations about Greater Manchester Mayor Andy Burnham’s London flat. The Labour figure purchased the ex-council property in Kennington during his time as an MP in 2005, with reports suggesting partial funding came through parliamentary expenses. The flat, now valued at approximately £480,000, has been rented out whilst Burnham claimed accommodation expenses for another property.

Housing Market Hypocrisy Claims

Conservative MP and former Hunters estate agency founder Kevin Hollinrake has criticised Burnham’s position, labelling it hypocritical given his public stance on housing affordability. Burnham has frequently spoken about growing wealth disparities between property owners and those struggling to enter the market. Hollinrake remarked: "The self-styled King of the North maintains a profitable London property portfolio whilst criticising the very system that enabled his investment."

This situation echoes the 2012 controversy when Burnham, then shadow health secretary, faced questions about claiming rental expenses whilst letting out his own property. The parliamentary expenses system underwent significant reform after the 2009 scandal, prohibiting MPs from claiming mortgage interest but still allowing London accommodation costs.

Right to Buy Paradox

The debate touches upon wider housing policy contradictions. Burnham has advocated for rent controls and called to suspend Right to Buy – the scheme allowing council tenants to purchase their homes at discounts. Ironically, his Kennington flat was originally acquired through Right to Buy before he purchased it. Housing analysts suggest such cases highlight the complex relationship individuals can have with property markets, where personal investments may not always align with public policy positions.

As housing remains a contentious political issue, the financial decisions of policymakers continue to attract public interest and scrutiny. Homeowners considering buy-to-let investments might reflect on how market participation intersects with broader housing affordability debates.

item.title: Propertymark welcomes leasehold ban proposal
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Potential leasehold ban could reshape flat ownership

The government's proposals to ban leasehold flats in England and Wales have gained support from industry experts, who suggest the move could create a fairer system for homeowners. Under the plans, most new-build flats would adopt a commonhold ownership model rather than traditional leasehold arrangements. This structural shift might give flat owners more control over building management whilst eliminating recurring costs like ground rent.

Why leasehold reform is being considered

Industry research indicates widespread dissatisfaction with leasehold properties, with over 90% of owners expressing regret about their purchase. Common concerns include unpredictable service charges, restrictive lease terms and opaque management practices. Estate agents report increasing difficulties in selling leasehold flats, suggesting the current model may be deterring potential buyers. The proposed reforms could make flat ownership more attractive to first-time buyers and property investors alike.

However, transition challenges must be carefully managed to avoid unintended consequences for existing leaseholders. Property professionals caution against mandatory conversion requirements that might create financial pressures. Instead, they advocate for phased implementation with proper protections for current homeowners. Clear guidance for buyers about commonhold obligations and rights would also be crucial as this alternative model becomes more prevalent.

The road ahead for property reforms

Alongside ownership structure changes, industry bodies are calling for tighter regulation of managing agents to improve transparency. These combined measures could help address systemic issues while maintaining market confidence. Homeowners considering purchasing leasehold properties might wish to research proposed changes carefully, whilst existing leaseholders may benefit from monitoring how protections for their situations develop. The government's consultation period offers an opportunity for stakeholders to shape this significant housing policy shift.

As these discussions progress, the property market could witness one of its most substantial transformations in decades – potentially redefining what flat ownership means for future generations of homeowners.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Potential leasehold ban could reshape flat ownership

The government's proposals to ban leasehold flats in England and Wales have gained support from industry experts, who suggest the move could create a fairer system for homeowners. Under the plans, most new-build flats would adopt a commonhold ownership model rather than traditional leasehold arrangements. This structural shift might give flat owners more control over building management whilst eliminating recurring costs like ground rent.

Why leasehold reform is being considered

Industry research indicates widespread dissatisfaction with leasehold properties, with over 90% of owners expressing regret about their purchase. Common concerns include unpredictable service charges, restrictive lease terms and opaque management practices. Estate agents report increasing difficulties in selling leasehold flats, suggesting the current model may be deterring potential buyers. The proposed reforms could make flat ownership more attractive to first-time buyers and property investors alike.

However, transition challenges must be carefully managed to avoid unintended consequences for existing leaseholders. Property professionals caution against mandatory conversion requirements that might create financial pressures. Instead, they advocate for phased implementation with proper protections for current homeowners. Clear guidance for buyers about commonhold obligations and rights would also be crucial as this alternative model becomes more prevalent.

The road ahead for property reforms

Alongside ownership structure changes, industry bodies are calling for tighter regulation of managing agents to improve transparency. These combined measures could help address systemic issues while maintaining market confidence. Homeowners considering purchasing leasehold properties might wish to research proposed changes carefully, whilst existing leaseholders may benefit from monitoring how protections for their situations develop. The government's consultation period offers an opportunity for stakeholders to shape this significant housing policy shift.

As these discussions progress, the property market could witness one of its most substantial transformations in decades – potentially redefining what flat ownership means for future generations of homeowners.

item.title: Andy Burnham vs the bond market – why housing could feel the impact
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Political shifts and the UK property market

Recent proposals for an additional council tax levy on high-value properties owned by non-UK residents have sparked debate about the potential impact on the housing market. The measure, initially introduced in November's Budget for properties valued over £2 million, could form part of broader political positioning as speculation grows about future leadership challenges within the Labour party.

Manchester mayor Andy Burnham's campaign for a parliamentary seat has brought attention to potential policy directions, with observers noting his historical preference for higher taxation and public spending. Whilst campaign rhetoric often differs from eventual policy, homeowners in premium markets might consider how political changes could influence property taxation and valuations.

International investment and prime market performance

The proposed surcharge has drawn criticism from business leaders who argue it sends mixed signals about the UK's openness to international investment. Leslie MacLeod-Miller of Foreign Investors for Britain suggests that existing measures, including high stamp duty rates and changes to non-dom status, already make the UK less competitive in attracting global wealth.

Data from prime central London appears to support these concerns, with LonRes figures showing an 18% decline in £5m+ property exchanges compared to the five-year average. Average prices in this segment have fallen 3.8% annually, continuing a three-year downward trend that leaves values 22% below their 2015 peak.

Balancing policy objectives

The debate highlights the challenge policymakers face in balancing revenue generation with maintaining the UK's appeal to high-net-worth individuals. Estate agents note that many overseas buyers in this price bracket are long-term residents who contribute significantly to local economies, rather than the absentee owners sometimes characterised in political discourse.

As political and economic landscapes evolve, those with interests in premium property markets might monitor how taxation policies develop, particularly in relation to international investment flows and domestic housing market performance.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Political shifts and the UK property market

Recent proposals for an additional council tax levy on high-value properties owned by non-UK residents have sparked debate about the potential impact on the housing market. The measure, initially introduced in November's Budget for properties valued over £2 million, could form part of broader political positioning as speculation grows about future leadership challenges within the Labour party.

Manchester mayor Andy Burnham's campaign for a parliamentary seat has brought attention to potential policy directions, with observers noting his historical preference for higher taxation and public spending. Whilst campaign rhetoric often differs from eventual policy, homeowners in premium markets might consider how political changes could influence property taxation and valuations.

International investment and prime market performance

The proposed surcharge has drawn criticism from business leaders who argue it sends mixed signals about the UK's openness to international investment. Leslie MacLeod-Miller of Foreign Investors for Britain suggests that existing measures, including high stamp duty rates and changes to non-dom status, already make the UK less competitive in attracting global wealth.

Data from prime central London appears to support these concerns, with LonRes figures showing an 18% decline in £5m+ property exchanges compared to the five-year average. Average prices in this segment have fallen 3.8% annually, continuing a three-year downward trend that leaves values 22% below their 2015 peak.

Balancing policy objectives

The debate highlights the challenge policymakers face in balancing revenue generation with maintaining the UK's appeal to high-net-worth individuals. Estate agents note that many overseas buyers in this price bracket are long-term residents who contribute significantly to local economies, rather than the absentee owners sometimes characterised in political discourse.

As political and economic landscapes evolve, those with interests in premium property markets might monitor how taxation policies develop, particularly in relation to international investment flows and domestic housing market performance.

item.title: Buy-to-let confidence holds firm as landlords take stock
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

New research suggests buy-to-let landlords are maintaining cautious optimism about their property portfolios despite wider economic uncertainties. The latest Landbay survey reveals most investors are adopting a measured approach, focusing on portfolio management rather than making sweeping changes to their strategies.

Landlords focus on controllable factors

The survey indicates landlords are concentrating on aspects within their direct control, with 41.4% describing their outlook as neutral regarding their own property businesses. Whilst 36.8% expressed negative sentiment about their portfolios, a significant 21.8% remained positive. This contrasts sharply with views on the UK economy overall, where 69.2% of respondents reported negative expectations.

Many landlords continue to achieve respectable rental yields, with 27.1% reporting gross returns between 4-6% and 21.8% achieving 6-8%. Notably, 15.8% of respondents said their yields exceed 10%, suggesting some portfolios are performing strongly despite market challenges.

Portfolio strategies show measured approach

Landlords appear to be taking a more selective approach to portfolio management. Over half (51.9%) said they don't plan to purchase additional properties in the near future, whilst 35.3% intend to expand their holdings. Selling intentions remain stable compared to previous surveys, indicating ongoing portfolio adjustments rather than mass exits from the sector.

Regarding rental pricing, more than three-quarters of landlords plan to increase rents in some form. However, many are reportedly considering tenant affordability and legislative changes when setting prices, suggesting a more flexible approach than in previous years.

Mortgage preferences remain consistent

Fixed-rate deals continue to dominate landlord preferences, with 87.2% likely to choose these products for their next mortgage. Five-year fixes proved most popular at 46.6%, followed by two and three-year terms. This preference for stability reflects landlords' focus on managing known costs amidst economic uncertainty.

The findings suggest the buy-to-let market is entering a period of careful recalibration, with experienced landlords making targeted decisions based on local market conditions rather than broad economic forecasts.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

New research suggests buy-to-let landlords are maintaining cautious optimism about their property portfolios despite wider economic uncertainties. The latest Landbay survey reveals most investors are adopting a measured approach, focusing on portfolio management rather than making sweeping changes to their strategies.

Landlords focus on controllable factors

The survey indicates landlords are concentrating on aspects within their direct control, with 41.4% describing their outlook as neutral regarding their own property businesses. Whilst 36.8% expressed negative sentiment about their portfolios, a significant 21.8% remained positive. This contrasts sharply with views on the UK economy overall, where 69.2% of respondents reported negative expectations.

Many landlords continue to achieve respectable rental yields, with 27.1% reporting gross returns between 4-6% and 21.8% achieving 6-8%. Notably, 15.8% of respondents said their yields exceed 10%, suggesting some portfolios are performing strongly despite market challenges.

Portfolio strategies show measured approach

Landlords appear to be taking a more selective approach to portfolio management. Over half (51.9%) said they don't plan to purchase additional properties in the near future, whilst 35.3% intend to expand their holdings. Selling intentions remain stable compared to previous surveys, indicating ongoing portfolio adjustments rather than mass exits from the sector.

Regarding rental pricing, more than three-quarters of landlords plan to increase rents in some form. However, many are reportedly considering tenant affordability and legislative changes when setting prices, suggesting a more flexible approach than in previous years.

Mortgage preferences remain consistent

Fixed-rate deals continue to dominate landlord preferences, with 87.2% likely to choose these products for their next mortgage. Five-year fixes proved most popular at 46.6%, followed by two and three-year terms. This preference for stability reflects landlords' focus on managing known costs amidst economic uncertainty.

The findings suggest the buy-to-let market is entering a period of careful recalibration, with experienced landlords making targeted decisions based on local market conditions rather than broad economic forecasts.

item.title: How UK Landlords Are Using Garden Cabins to Boost Rental Yield: A 2026 Analysis
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

UK landlords have faced significant financial pressures over the past three years, with changes to mortgage interest tax relief, rising interest rates, and tighter regulations under the Renters' Rights legislation. In response, many are exploring creative ways to maximise rental income, with garden cabins emerging as a popular solution for boosting yields.

The Appeal of Garden Cabins for Landlords

As traditional buy-to-let margins shrink, property investors are increasingly turning to alternative strategies to enhance returns. Garden cabins, often referred to as "garden rooms" or "annexes," offer several advantages:

  • Lower upfront costs compared to extensions or loft conversions
  • Faster installation with minimal planning permission requirements in most cases
  • Potential to create additional living space or home offices, appealing to remote workers
  • Opportunity to charge premium rents for properties with extra facilities

Financial Considerations for Landlords

Whilst garden cabins can improve rental yields, landlords might consider several financial factors before investing:

  • Mortgage implications - some lenders may require notification or consent for outbuildings
  • Insurance requirements - additional structures may need specific coverage
  • Tax treatment - different rules may apply to capital improvements versus repairs
  • Local authority regulations - permitted development rights vary across councils

With careful planning and professional advice, garden cabins could represent a viable option for landlords looking to enhance their rental income in today's challenging market. However, it remains crucial to weigh the costs against potential returns and ensure all legal requirements are met.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

UK landlords have faced significant financial pressures over the past three years, with changes to mortgage interest tax relief, rising interest rates, and tighter regulations under the Renters' Rights legislation. In response, many are exploring creative ways to maximise rental income, with garden cabins emerging as a popular solution for boosting yields.

The Appeal of Garden Cabins for Landlords

As traditional buy-to-let margins shrink, property investors are increasingly turning to alternative strategies to enhance returns. Garden cabins, often referred to as "garden rooms" or "annexes," offer several advantages:

  • Lower upfront costs compared to extensions or loft conversions
  • Faster installation with minimal planning permission requirements in most cases
  • Potential to create additional living space or home offices, appealing to remote workers
  • Opportunity to charge premium rents for properties with extra facilities

Financial Considerations for Landlords

Whilst garden cabins can improve rental yields, landlords might consider several financial factors before investing:

  • Mortgage implications - some lenders may require notification or consent for outbuildings
  • Insurance requirements - additional structures may need specific coverage
  • Tax treatment - different rules may apply to capital improvements versus repairs
  • Local authority regulations - permitted development rights vary across councils

With careful planning and professional advice, garden cabins could represent a viable option for landlords looking to enhance their rental income in today's challenging market. However, it remains crucial to weigh the costs against potential returns and ensure all legal requirements are met.

item.title: What is currently happening in the UK property market?
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK property market continues to show resilience in 2026, with recent weekly data revealing interesting trends for both buyers and sellers. Unlike traditional indices that operate on significant time lags, live market tracking provides a more immediate picture of housing activity across the nation.

Current Market Activity

Week 19 of 2026 saw 41,800 new property listings enter the market – a notable increase from 34,600 in the previous week, which was affected by a bank holiday. This figure sits comfortably above both the 2026 weekly average of 37,500 and the ten-year benchmark of 32,300 for the same period. Year-to-date listings currently stand at 713,000, showing modest growth compared to 2025 levels.

Sales activity also demonstrated positive movement, with 27,000 homes going under offer in Week 19. This represents an improvement from 22,500 in Week 18 and exceeds the ten-year average of 24,000 for this period. However, the year-to-date total of 471,000 sold properties remains 5.3% below 2025 levels, suggesting some market cooling compared to last year's performance.

Regional Variations and Market Health

When examining net sales (gross sales minus fall-throughs), Week 19 recorded 21,800 completed transactions – up from 17,500 in the previous week. The 2026 weekly average currently stands at 19,400, slightly higher than 2025's full-year average of 18,800. Year-to-date net sales of 369,000 properties show the market remains stronger than both 2023 and pre-pandemic levels, though slightly below 2025 figures.

Local market analysis continues to provide valuable insights for both homeowners and industry professionals. Recent focus on areas like Scunthorpe has highlighted variations in pricing strategies, time-to-sell metrics and agent performance – factors that can significantly influence regional market dynamics.

While the market shows overall strength, potential buyers and sellers might wish to consider these live indicators alongside their personal circumstances. The data suggests a property landscape that, while not matching 2025's peak activity, remains significantly more active than pre-pandemic norms.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK property market continues to show resilience in 2026, with recent weekly data revealing interesting trends for both buyers and sellers. Unlike traditional indices that operate on significant time lags, live market tracking provides a more immediate picture of housing activity across the nation.

Current Market Activity

Week 19 of 2026 saw 41,800 new property listings enter the market – a notable increase from 34,600 in the previous week, which was affected by a bank holiday. This figure sits comfortably above both the 2026 weekly average of 37,500 and the ten-year benchmark of 32,300 for the same period. Year-to-date listings currently stand at 713,000, showing modest growth compared to 2025 levels.

Sales activity also demonstrated positive movement, with 27,000 homes going under offer in Week 19. This represents an improvement from 22,500 in Week 18 and exceeds the ten-year average of 24,000 for this period. However, the year-to-date total of 471,000 sold properties remains 5.3% below 2025 levels, suggesting some market cooling compared to last year's performance.

Regional Variations and Market Health

When examining net sales (gross sales minus fall-throughs), Week 19 recorded 21,800 completed transactions – up from 17,500 in the previous week. The 2026 weekly average currently stands at 19,400, slightly higher than 2025's full-year average of 18,800. Year-to-date net sales of 369,000 properties show the market remains stronger than both 2023 and pre-pandemic levels, though slightly below 2025 figures.

Local market analysis continues to provide valuable insights for both homeowners and industry professionals. Recent focus on areas like Scunthorpe has highlighted variations in pricing strategies, time-to-sell metrics and agent performance – factors that can significantly influence regional market dynamics.

While the market shows overall strength, potential buyers and sellers might wish to consider these live indicators alongside their personal circumstances. The data suggests a property landscape that, while not matching 2025's peak activity, remains significantly more active than pre-pandemic norms.

item.title: Labour government is not working – the Renters’ Rights Bill proves it
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The Renters’ Rights Bill, introduced by the Labour government, has sparked significant debate across the UK property market. While the legislation was marketed as a win for tenants, its unintended consequences have raised concerns among landlords, renters, and industry experts alike. With a shrinking supply of rental properties and rising rents, the bill’s impact on the housing market has become a focal point of discussion.

The Impact on Rental Supply

One of the most notable effects of the Renters’ Rights Bill has been the exodus of private landlords from the buy-to-let market. Many landlords have chosen to sell their properties rather than navigate the new regulations, which they perceive as overly restrictive. This reduction in available rental homes has created a supply crunch, particularly in areas already facing housing shortages.

As a result, tenants—especially vulnerable families, young workers, and those on lower incomes—are finding it increasingly difficult to secure affordable housing. With fewer properties on the market, competition has intensified, driving rents upward. This outcome appears to contradict the bill’s original aim of improving conditions for renters.

Broader Economic Concerns

The Renters’ Rights Bill is not the only policy causing ripples in the economy. The introduction of the Workers’ Rights Bill, alongside increases in National Insurance contributions and the minimum wage, has led to concerns about the cost of employment. Many businesses now face higher operational expenses, which could discourage hiring and investment.

These measures, while intended to protect workers, may inadvertently reduce job opportunities, particularly for younger people entering the workforce. Critics argue that such policies could lead to higher unemployment and stifle economic growth, ultimately harming the very individuals they were designed to support.

A Complex Policy Landscape

The Renters’ Rights Bill highlights the challenges of balancing tenant protections with the need to maintain a healthy rental market. Whilst the intentions behind the legislation may be commendable, its implementation has raised questions about the broader impact on housing affordability and availability.

Homeowners, landlords, and policymakers alike may wish to consider the long-term implications of such reforms. Addressing the housing crisis requires a nuanced approach that supports both renters and landlords, ensuring a stable and sustainable rental market for the future.

As the UK navigates these complex policy changes, the need for careful evaluation and collaboration between stakeholders remains crucial. Striking the right balance between tenant rights and market dynamics will be key to fostering a fair and functional housing sector.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The Renters’ Rights Bill, introduced by the Labour government, has sparked significant debate across the UK property market. While the legislation was marketed as a win for tenants, its unintended consequences have raised concerns among landlords, renters, and industry experts alike. With a shrinking supply of rental properties and rising rents, the bill’s impact on the housing market has become a focal point of discussion.

The Impact on Rental Supply

One of the most notable effects of the Renters’ Rights Bill has been the exodus of private landlords from the buy-to-let market. Many landlords have chosen to sell their properties rather than navigate the new regulations, which they perceive as overly restrictive. This reduction in available rental homes has created a supply crunch, particularly in areas already facing housing shortages.

As a result, tenants—especially vulnerable families, young workers, and those on lower incomes—are finding it increasingly difficult to secure affordable housing. With fewer properties on the market, competition has intensified, driving rents upward. This outcome appears to contradict the bill’s original aim of improving conditions for renters.

Broader Economic Concerns

The Renters’ Rights Bill is not the only policy causing ripples in the economy. The introduction of the Workers’ Rights Bill, alongside increases in National Insurance contributions and the minimum wage, has led to concerns about the cost of employment. Many businesses now face higher operational expenses, which could discourage hiring and investment.

These measures, while intended to protect workers, may inadvertently reduce job opportunities, particularly for younger people entering the workforce. Critics argue that such policies could lead to higher unemployment and stifle economic growth, ultimately harming the very individuals they were designed to support.

A Complex Policy Landscape

The Renters’ Rights Bill highlights the challenges of balancing tenant protections with the need to maintain a healthy rental market. Whilst the intentions behind the legislation may be commendable, its implementation has raised questions about the broader impact on housing affordability and availability.

Homeowners, landlords, and policymakers alike may wish to consider the long-term implications of such reforms. Addressing the housing crisis requires a nuanced approach that supports both renters and landlords, ensuring a stable and sustainable rental market for the future.

As the UK navigates these complex policy changes, the need for careful evaluation and collaboration between stakeholders remains crucial. Striking the right balance between tenant rights and market dynamics will be key to fostering a fair and functional housing sector.

item.title: Landlords yet to rush for exits following Section 21 eviction ban
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Recent data from the Ministry of Justice indicates that landlords have not yet rushed to initiate possession claims ahead of the abolition of Section 21 evictions. Whilst the Renters’ Rights Bill, which will end so-called “no-fault” evictions, has sparked concerns among landlords, the anticipated surge in court proceedings has not fully materialised. However, experts suggest it may still be too early to gauge the full impact of these reforms.

Current Possession Trends

The first quarter of the year saw a decline in possession activity across both the mortgage and rental sectors compared to the same period in 2023. Mortgage possession claims dropped by 23% year-on-year, from 6,767 to 5,181, while landlord possession claims fell by 5%, from 23,964 to 22,733. Repossessions carried out by county court bailiffs remained relatively stable, with mortgage repossessions at 1,101 and landlord repossessions decreasing by 6% to 6,888.

Regionally, mortgage possession claims declined across all areas, whilst landlord claims fell everywhere except Wales, where they saw a slight increase. Despite these reductions, court delays continue to worsen. The median time from mortgage possession claim to repossession rose to 45.7 weeks, up from 40.5 weeks in the previous year. For landlords, the average time from claim to repossession increased slightly to 26.4 weeks.

Landlords Preparing for Reform

Experts believe many landlords are reassessing their positions in the market ahead of the Renters’ Rights Bill coming into force. Some may be accelerating possession proceedings or considering whether to retain or sell their properties before the reforms take effect. Concerns remain over court capacity and how quickly landlords will be able to regain possession once Section 21 is abolished.

Paul Shamplina, founder of Landlord Action, noted that whilst the Q1 figures do not show a dramatic spike in possession claims, the data suggests ongoing pressure within the private rented sector. He highlighted that there is often a lag between landlords serving notice and cases reaching the court claim stage, meaning the full impact of the reforms may not yet be reflected in the statistics.

As the abolition of Section 21 approaches, landlords might consider seeking professional advice to navigate the changing landscape. Homeowners and investors alike could benefit from staying informed about the potential implications of these reforms on the property market.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Recent data from the Ministry of Justice indicates that landlords have not yet rushed to initiate possession claims ahead of the abolition of Section 21 evictions. Whilst the Renters’ Rights Bill, which will end so-called “no-fault” evictions, has sparked concerns among landlords, the anticipated surge in court proceedings has not fully materialised. However, experts suggest it may still be too early to gauge the full impact of these reforms.

Current Possession Trends

The first quarter of the year saw a decline in possession activity across both the mortgage and rental sectors compared to the same period in 2023. Mortgage possession claims dropped by 23% year-on-year, from 6,767 to 5,181, while landlord possession claims fell by 5%, from 23,964 to 22,733. Repossessions carried out by county court bailiffs remained relatively stable, with mortgage repossessions at 1,101 and landlord repossessions decreasing by 6% to 6,888.

Regionally, mortgage possession claims declined across all areas, whilst landlord claims fell everywhere except Wales, where they saw a slight increase. Despite these reductions, court delays continue to worsen. The median time from mortgage possession claim to repossession rose to 45.7 weeks, up from 40.5 weeks in the previous year. For landlords, the average time from claim to repossession increased slightly to 26.4 weeks.

Landlords Preparing for Reform

Experts believe many landlords are reassessing their positions in the market ahead of the Renters’ Rights Bill coming into force. Some may be accelerating possession proceedings or considering whether to retain or sell their properties before the reforms take effect. Concerns remain over court capacity and how quickly landlords will be able to regain possession once Section 21 is abolished.

Paul Shamplina, founder of Landlord Action, noted that whilst the Q1 figures do not show a dramatic spike in possession claims, the data suggests ongoing pressure within the private rented sector. He highlighted that there is often a lag between landlords serving notice and cases reaching the court claim stage, meaning the full impact of the reforms may not yet be reflected in the statistics.

As the abolition of Section 21 approaches, landlords might consider seeking professional advice to navigate the changing landscape. Homeowners and investors alike could benefit from staying informed about the potential implications of these reforms on the property market.

item.title: Ex-housing secretary Angela Rayner drawn into ‘election fraud’ row as arrests made
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Former housing secretary Angela Rayner has become embroiled in controversy following allegations of electoral fraud in her Ashton-under-Lyne constituency. Greater Manchester Police arrested five individuals last week as part of an investigation into claims that fake independent candidates were placed on ballot papers during May's local elections.

Election fraud investigation underway

Police confirmed they detained four men and one woman, aged between 23 and 47, on suspicion of conspiracy to defraud. The allegations centre on Tameside borough council elections, where concerns were raised about the nomination process in St Peter's ward. Labour candidate Atta ul-Rasool secured victory by 177 votes, while two allegedly bogus independent candidates received a combined 291 votes.

Investigators are examining whether electoral procedures were properly followed when candidates were nominated. The Electoral Commission is assisting with enquiries, which focus on potential attempts to split opposition votes through the inclusion of unauthorised independent candidates.

Labour denies Rayner's involvement

Reports have suggested discussions about the alleged tactic occurred among local Labour figures, with one conversation purportedly taking place in Rayner's kitchen. However, sources close to the former deputy Labour leader insist she had no knowledge of or involvement in any wrongdoing.

A Labour Party spokesperson stated no evidence had been presented to suggest party involvement in electoral malpractice. The controversy emerges as Labour prepares for a crucial by-election in nearby Makerfield, where the party faces increasing political scrutiny across Greater Manchester.

Greater Manchester Police continue their investigation, working with electoral authorities to determine whether any laws were broken during the nomination process. The force has urged anyone with relevant information to come forward as enquiries progress.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Former housing secretary Angela Rayner has become embroiled in controversy following allegations of electoral fraud in her Ashton-under-Lyne constituency. Greater Manchester Police arrested five individuals last week as part of an investigation into claims that fake independent candidates were placed on ballot papers during May's local elections.

Election fraud investigation underway

Police confirmed they detained four men and one woman, aged between 23 and 47, on suspicion of conspiracy to defraud. The allegations centre on Tameside borough council elections, where concerns were raised about the nomination process in St Peter's ward. Labour candidate Atta ul-Rasool secured victory by 177 votes, while two allegedly bogus independent candidates received a combined 291 votes.

Investigators are examining whether electoral procedures were properly followed when candidates were nominated. The Electoral Commission is assisting with enquiries, which focus on potential attempts to split opposition votes through the inclusion of unauthorised independent candidates.

Labour denies Rayner's involvement

Reports have suggested discussions about the alleged tactic occurred among local Labour figures, with one conversation purportedly taking place in Rayner's kitchen. However, sources close to the former deputy Labour leader insist she had no knowledge of or involvement in any wrongdoing.

A Labour Party spokesperson stated no evidence had been presented to suggest party involvement in electoral malpractice. The controversy emerges as Labour prepares for a crucial by-election in nearby Makerfield, where the party faces increasing political scrutiny across Greater Manchester.

Greater Manchester Police continue their investigation, working with electoral authorities to determine whether any laws were broken during the nomination process. The force has urged anyone with relevant information to come forward as enquiries progress.

item.title: Landlord Law Newsround #437
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Welsh landlords face significant changes to tenancy agreements under new legislation coming into force in 2026, whilst research suggests property owners increasingly favour tenants who vape over smokers. Meanwhile, local authorities are taking a tougher stance on landlord compliance, with immediate fines for breaches becoming more common.

Welsh Tenancy Law Changes Coming in 2026

From 1st June 2026, Welsh landlords will be prohibited from discriminating against tenants with children or those receiving benefits. The new rules require landlords to update standard occupation contracts and serve notices to existing tenants by 14th June 2026. Those failing to comply could face compensation claims equivalent to two months' rent from affected tenants.

Propertymark's Tim Thomas notes these changes may create administrative challenges for landlords, particularly given the tight deadlines for updating contracts. The Welsh government is also reportedly considering further reforms, including potential bans on 'no fault' evictions and rental bidding wars.

Landlords Show Preference for Vaping Tenants

Recent research indicates landlords generally view vaping tenants more favourably than smokers, with fewer reported incidents and lower associated costs. Whilst smoking-related breaches typically trigger stronger reactions from landlords (39% vs 30% for vaping), the financial impact appears comparable — averaging £107 per property for smoking versus £102 for vaping in cleaning and deodorising expenses.

  • Some landlords report smoking damage costing up to £500 per property
  • Clear tenancy agreements regarding smoking policies remain essential
  • Vaping seen as causing less property damage and fewer neighbour complaints

Councils Adopt Zero-Tolerance Approach to Compliance

Local authorities now have enhanced powers to issue immediate fines for housing violations, with penalties reaching £40,000 for serious breaches. Although councils must technically issue a Notice of Intent before imposing fines, the window for landlords to respond is often extremely limited.

Property compliance expert Phil Turtle advises landlords to proactively review their obligations rather than waiting for enforcement action. Maintaining up-to-date compliance records and having professional contacts readily available could help landlords avoid substantial penalties.

With regulatory changes accelerating across the private rental sector, landlords may wish to stay informed about evolving requirements in their area. Keeping tenancy agreements current and maintaining properties to standard could help avoid both financial penalties and tenant disputes.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Welsh landlords face significant changes to tenancy agreements under new legislation coming into force in 2026, whilst research suggests property owners increasingly favour tenants who vape over smokers. Meanwhile, local authorities are taking a tougher stance on landlord compliance, with immediate fines for breaches becoming more common.

Welsh Tenancy Law Changes Coming in 2026

From 1st June 2026, Welsh landlords will be prohibited from discriminating against tenants with children or those receiving benefits. The new rules require landlords to update standard occupation contracts and serve notices to existing tenants by 14th June 2026. Those failing to comply could face compensation claims equivalent to two months' rent from affected tenants.

Propertymark's Tim Thomas notes these changes may create administrative challenges for landlords, particularly given the tight deadlines for updating contracts. The Welsh government is also reportedly considering further reforms, including potential bans on 'no fault' evictions and rental bidding wars.

Landlords Show Preference for Vaping Tenants

Recent research indicates landlords generally view vaping tenants more favourably than smokers, with fewer reported incidents and lower associated costs. Whilst smoking-related breaches typically trigger stronger reactions from landlords (39% vs 30% for vaping), the financial impact appears comparable — averaging £107 per property for smoking versus £102 for vaping in cleaning and deodorising expenses.

  • Some landlords report smoking damage costing up to £500 per property
  • Clear tenancy agreements regarding smoking policies remain essential
  • Vaping seen as causing less property damage and fewer neighbour complaints

Councils Adopt Zero-Tolerance Approach to Compliance

Local authorities now have enhanced powers to issue immediate fines for housing violations, with penalties reaching £40,000 for serious breaches. Although councils must technically issue a Notice of Intent before imposing fines, the window for landlords to respond is often extremely limited.

Property compliance expert Phil Turtle advises landlords to proactively review their obligations rather than waiting for enforcement action. Maintaining up-to-date compliance records and having professional contacts readily available could help landlords avoid substantial penalties.

With regulatory changes accelerating across the private rental sector, landlords may wish to stay informed about evolving requirements in their area. Keeping tenancy agreements current and maintaining properties to standard could help avoid both financial penalties and tenant disputes.

item.title: Government warned against ‘one of the biggest own goals’ in housing policy history
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The government’s proposed leasehold reforms are sparking debate after new research revealed high levels of satisfaction among leaseholders, casting doubt on the need for sweeping changes. The latest English Housing Survey found that 93% of leaseholders living in flats are content with their tenure, marking the first large-scale study of its kind. These findings have prompted warnings from industry experts, who argue that the reforms could have unintended consequences for both leaseholders and freeholders.

Leasehold Reforms Under Scrutiny

The proposed Commonhold and Leasehold Reform Bill aims to overhaul the leasehold system, including measures to remove ground rent income received by professional freeholders. Freeholders are responsible for maintaining and managing residential buildings, ensuring safety compliance and upkeep. However, critics argue that these changes could destabilise the sector, potentially leaving some freeholders insolvent and creating uncertainty about who would assume responsibility for affected properties.

The Residential Freehold Association (RFA) has raised concerns that leaseholders could be forced to take on the legal duties and liabilities of managing their buildings, regardless of their views on the reforms. Natalie Chambers, director of the RFA, highlighted that the government’s own research shows widespread satisfaction with the current system, urging ministers to reconsider the proposed changes.

Financial Implications of the Reforms

The Ministry of Housing’s impact assessment acknowledges that no formal work has been done to quantify the potential wealth redistribution resulting from the reforms. Meanwhile, economic analysis commissioned by the sector estimates that the proposals could remove approximately £18.7bn in ground rent investment value. A significant portion of the financial benefits would reportedly go to property investors in London and the South East, which account for around 55% of the projected gains.

Critics argue that the reforms could disproportionately affect freeholders while failing to address the needs of leaseholders, many of whom are satisfied with the current system. The RFA warns that the changes could lead to a “redistributive transfer of wealth” without clear plans for ensuring the ongoing maintenance and safety of buildings.

As the debate continues, homeowners and industry stakeholders may wish to stay informed about the potential implications of the proposed reforms. With high satisfaction levels among leaseholders and significant financial concerns raised by freeholders, the government’s approach to leasehold reform remains a contentious issue.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The government’s proposed leasehold reforms are sparking debate after new research revealed high levels of satisfaction among leaseholders, casting doubt on the need for sweeping changes. The latest English Housing Survey found that 93% of leaseholders living in flats are content with their tenure, marking the first large-scale study of its kind. These findings have prompted warnings from industry experts, who argue that the reforms could have unintended consequences for both leaseholders and freeholders.

Leasehold Reforms Under Scrutiny

The proposed Commonhold and Leasehold Reform Bill aims to overhaul the leasehold system, including measures to remove ground rent income received by professional freeholders. Freeholders are responsible for maintaining and managing residential buildings, ensuring safety compliance and upkeep. However, critics argue that these changes could destabilise the sector, potentially leaving some freeholders insolvent and creating uncertainty about who would assume responsibility for affected properties.

The Residential Freehold Association (RFA) has raised concerns that leaseholders could be forced to take on the legal duties and liabilities of managing their buildings, regardless of their views on the reforms. Natalie Chambers, director of the RFA, highlighted that the government’s own research shows widespread satisfaction with the current system, urging ministers to reconsider the proposed changes.

Financial Implications of the Reforms

The Ministry of Housing’s impact assessment acknowledges that no formal work has been done to quantify the potential wealth redistribution resulting from the reforms. Meanwhile, economic analysis commissioned by the sector estimates that the proposals could remove approximately £18.7bn in ground rent investment value. A significant portion of the financial benefits would reportedly go to property investors in London and the South East, which account for around 55% of the projected gains.

Critics argue that the reforms could disproportionately affect freeholders while failing to address the needs of leaseholders, many of whom are satisfied with the current system. The RFA warns that the changes could lead to a “redistributive transfer of wealth” without clear plans for ensuring the ongoing maintenance and safety of buildings.

As the debate continues, homeowners and industry stakeholders may wish to stay informed about the potential implications of the proposed reforms. With high satisfaction levels among leaseholders and significant financial concerns raised by freeholders, the government’s approach to leasehold reform remains a contentious issue.

item.title: Scrap stamp duty and council tax to tackle housing crisis
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

A leading housing thinktank has proposed significant reforms to the UK’s property tax system, suggesting the abolition of stamp duty and council tax in favour of an annual property wealth tax. The Centre for London argues that these changes could help tackle the capital’s housing crisis by encouraging older homeowners to downsize, improving the use of existing housing stock, and generating additional funds for social housing. The reforms could also make it easier for renters to save for deposits by reducing upfront moving costs.

Addressing Inequality in Housing Space

The report highlights stark inequalities in housing space across London. While the average Londoner now has more living space than two decades ago, the distribution of this space is uneven. Households in the top 20% of income brackets have seen their living space increase by 27% since 2004, compared to just 6% for the bottom 40% of earners. This imbalance reflects broader structural issues in London’s housing market, where rising property wealth among higher-income households has coincided with increasing affordability pressures and record levels of temporary accommodation.

Rob Anderson, director of research at the Centre for London and co-author of the report, stated, “By every metric that matters, the housing crisis is at its worst.” The proposed reforms aim to address these issues by introducing a proportional property tax (PPT), which would replace stamp duty and council tax. Under this system, homeowners with larger properties in more expensive areas would pay the most tax. The thinktank estimates that the PPT could fund the construction of 106,000 social and affordable homes over the next decade.
